Client 2: And I’m Michael. I’m more the month-to-month guy, so I just want to make sure we’re not overcommitting.
Advisor: That makes sense. I’ll keep this pretty practical today—get the big numbers right, then we can talk priorities. First, at the household level, what do you consider your gross income for the year—before taxes?
Client 1: Combined, our household gross income (annual) is 52600.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m splitting it correctly, Patricia, you’re self-employed and Michael, you’re employed, right?
Client 1: Yes—self employed for me.

Client 2: We’re around two-ish, maybe twenty-four hundred most months.
Advisor: Okay—ballpark helps. If you had to pin it to one number for planning, what would you use as your total monthly expenses?
Client 1: Total monthly expenses are 2350.
Advisor: Perfect. Next, debt—what are your monthly debt payments total? Things like car loans, student loans, credit cards—minimums, anything like that.
Client 2: We don’t have any of that right now.

Client 2: And I’m Michael. I’m more the month-to-month guy, so I just want to make sure we’re not overcommitting.
Advisor: That makes sense. I’ll keep this pretty practical today—get the big numbers right, then we can talk priorities. First, at the household level, what do you consider your gross income for the year—before taxes?

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m splitting it correctly, Patricia, you’re self-employed and Michael, you’re employed, right?
Client 1: Yes—self employed for me.

Client 1: Yes—monthly debt payments (total) are 0.
Advisor: Great. Let’s talk assets next—what you’ve got saved and invested. If you add up the investable accounts—brokerage, retirement, cash—what’s the total investable assets number you’d want me to use?
Client 1: Investable assets (total) are 277950.
Advisor: Thanks. I’m going to break that into the actual buckets so we don’t miss anything. What do you have in a joint taxable investment account—like a brokerage account you can access anytime?
Client 2: The joint taxable one is 128050.

Client 1: That one is 16150.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. And then home: do you own your primary residence together, and what value should we use for it?
Client 2: Yes, jointly. The primary residence value is 169200.
Advisor: Helpful. On the income side, I also want to capture the sources. Patricia, for your business income line—what’s the annual amount you want on file, even if it comes in irregularly?
Client 1: For the business income line, the annual amount is 13400, and it’s ad hoc.

Client 2: And I’m Michael. I’m more the month-to-month guy, so I just want to make sure we’re not overcommitting.

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m splitting it correctly, Patricia, you’re self-employed and Michael, you’re employed, right?
Client 1: Yes—self employed for me.

Advisor: Hi Patricia, hi Michael—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Patricia. Honestly, it’s the uneven timing of my business income—I want a plan that still works even when cash flow is lumpy.
Client 2: And I’m Michael. I’m more the month-to-month guy, so I just want to make sure we’re not overcommitting.
Advisor: That makes sense. I’ll keep this pretty practical today—get the big numbers right, then we can talk priorities. First, at the household level, what do you consider your gross income for the year—before taxes?
Client 1: Combined, our household gross income (annual) is 52600.

Client 2: And I’m Michael. I’m more the month-to-month guy, so I just want to make sure we’re not overcommitting.
Advisor: That makes sense. I’ll keep this pretty practical today—get the big numbers right, then we can talk priorities. First, at the household level, what do you consider your gross income for the year—before taxes?
Client 1: Combined, our household gross income (annual) is 52600.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m splitting it correctly, Patricia, you’re self-employed and Michael, you’re employed, right?
Client 1: Yes—self employed for me.

Advisor: Okay—thank you. And protection-wise, just to close that loop: still no life, disability, or long-term care policies in place right now for either of you?
Client 1: Correct, none right now.
Advisor: Perfect. Next time we can talk through what coverage would actually matter given the business income variability, but for today I think we’ve got the baseline.
Advisor: Okay—thanks, Patricia, thanks Michael. Before we wrap today, I want to confirm the household basics so I’m not making assumptions.
Advisor: In the household, is it just the two of you—how many adults are we counting?

Advisor: Great—thank you. Michael, does that match what you were expecting too?
Client 2: Yeah, that’s fine. Ongoing makes sense as long as we keep it pretty practical month to month.
Advisor: Totally. Last piece for today is risk—just the plain-English version. When markets swing, do you see yourselves as conservative, moderate, or more growth-oriented?
Client 2: I’m probably more moderate—like I don’t love big drops.
Advisor: Yep, that makes sense. Patricia, if you had to pick the overall setting for the household—what should I put down?

Advisor: Okay—before we actually hit stop, I realized we didn’t lock down a couple household basics that matter for taxes and planning. Patricia, Michael—can I grab those quickly?
Client 1: Yeah, go ahead.
Advisor: First one: marital status. Are you two legally married, or partnered another way?
Client 2: We’re married.
Advisor: Got it. Next—country for your primary residence for planning purposes. Are you based in the US?

Advisor: Hi Patricia, hi Michael—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Patricia. Honestly, it’s the uneven timing of my business income—I want a plan that still works even when cash flow is lumpy.
Client 2: And I’m Michael. I’m more the month-to-month guy, so I just want to make sure we’re not overcommitting.
Advisor: That makes sense. I’ll keep this pretty practical today—get the big numbers right, then we can talk priorities. First, at the household level, what do you consider your gross income for the year—before taxes?

Advisor: Great—thanks. I want to tighten up the debt piece just a bit, Patricia and Michael. You said no loans outstanding—does that include the house, meaning no mortgage at all?
Client 2: Yeah, no mortgage. Mortgage balance outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Got it. And just to separate it: any other debt at all—credit cards you carry, car loan, student loans—anything non-mortgage?
Client 1: No—non-mortgage debt outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Perfect. And then on the property side—just confirming the value we’re using for the home, the total property value for planning—what number should I anchor to?

Client 2: I always mix this up—are you asking like the percent?
Advisor: Yeah—just the bracket percentage you generally fall into. Doesn’t have to be perfect, but what do you use as the working assumption?
Client 1: Use 32%.
Advisor: Great—thank you. And just so I’m consistent with what we already captured today: income lines were your business income and Michael’s salary, assets included the taxable account, your retirement accounts, the bank cash, and the home, liabilities were zero, and no protection policies right now. Sound right?
Client 2: Yep, that matches.

Client 2: Yep, and I’m Client 2.
Advisor: Perfect. Patricia, what should I use for your gross annual income—just the annual number before taxes?
Client 1: My gross annual income is 34650.
Advisor: Thank you. And last one on you, Patricia—how would you describe your role at work? Like executive, manager, technical, that kind of bucket.
Client 1: Executive—so, exec.
